response:
## Decision

**ADR 0045 — Sync-before-spend stays prose (strengthened, two checkpoints); claim protocol deferred.** Full record: `docs/decisions/0045-sync-before-spend-not-claim-protocol.md`. Rule shipped in `CLAUDE.md` under a new `## Session ops` section.

**Verdict:** the prose rule is enough for now — but not as originally drafted. A single checkpoint ("sync before executing") doesn't close the gap the incident actually showed: the clone was fresh-at-*dispatch*, stale-by-*use* across the run. The rule now has two checkpoints — resync + re-read the issue/linked PRs immediately before executing, **and again immediately before the final push**. I did not build the claim protocol.

**Why not mechanize now:**
- One observed incident, bounded cost (~$5-8), and it self-corrected via the session's own end-of-run `git fetch` — this is a rare, not-yet-recurring failure mode, not a standing pattern.
- The claim protocol is asymmetric-worse, not just more expensive: it needs issue-write permission that dispatch instructions sometimes withhold (per the issue itself), so mandating it fails exactly in the sessions where isolation is weakest; and a session that dies mid-run leaves a stale "in progress" claim that blocks *legitimate* future work — a worse, harder-to-notice failure than the one it prevents.
- There's no dispatch/backlog skill in this repo today to host a scripted gate — mechanizing the sync check has nowhere shipped to live yet (verified: no `dispatch` hit anywhere in `skills/*/SKILL.md` or `README.md`).
- Matches this repo's own "don't gold-plate — premature process machinery is itself muda" (`CLAUDE.md:21`).

**Revisit triggers** (recorded in the ADR): a second duplicate-spend despite both checkpoints; multi-session dispatch becoming a standing frequent practice rather than incidental; or a dispatch skill landing that gives the check a natural shipped home to graduate into a real gate.

The related foreground-wait lesson (detached subagent runs stalling) is housed in the same `## Session ops` section per the issue's request.

One loose end: `ISSUE-106.md` at the repo root now duplicates what's captured in ADR 0045 (decision-state) and presumably a real GitHub issue #106 (work-state) — `CLAUDE.md`'s own rule bars handoff/TODO files in the repo. I left it in place since it's your input artifact, not mine to remove unilaterally — let me know if you want it deleted now that the decision is recorded.

--- recorded decision file docs/decisions/0045-sync-before-spend-not-claim-protocol.md ---
---
id: 0045
title: "Sync-before-spend stays prose, two checkpoints; claim protocol deferred"
status: accepted
summary: "Issue #106's duplicate-spend (issue #30 executed twice in parallel, ~$5-8) gets a
strengthened prose sync-before-spend rule (CLAUDE.md, two checkpoints: before executing AND
before final push) rather than a mechanized issue-claim protocol. One observed incident,
self-detected via existing fetch, bounded cost; the claim protocol trades a rare bounded loss for
a worse one (permission-gated, stale-lock risk) with no shipped dispatch surface to host a gate.
Revisit on recurrence or a dispatch skill landing."
---

# 0045 — Sync-before-spend stays prose, two checkpoints; claim protocol deferred

- Date: 2026-07-14
- Owner: PM
- Panel: lean-process-engineer (waste vs poka-yoke), process-economist (cost-per-decision),
  session-operator (live-session realism) — unanimous accept.
- Context: ISSUE-106.md:6 — a backlog session executed issue #30 in full (~$5-8 nested-call +
  agent spend) while `main` already carried PR #97's merged fix; the clone was fresh-at-start but
  stale-by-use, only caught at end-of-session `git fetch`. ISSUE-106.md:8-10 names the gap: nothing
  forces a re-sync immediately before spend, and no visible claim marks an issue in-progress.
  ISSUE-106.md:12-18 offers three candidates: prose sync rule, a claim protocol (issue comment),
  or both.

## Decision
Strengthen the sync-before-spend rule and ship it as prose in CLAUDE.md now (`## Session ops`,
this ADR); do not build a claim protocol. The rule gets two checkpoints, not one: re-sync +
re-read the issue/linked PRs immediately before executing (not just at dispatch) AND again
immediately before the final push — closing the exact gap the incident showed (stale-by-use
during a run, not stale-at-start). House the related foreground-wait lesson
(ISSUE-106.md:18) in the same section, per the issue's own request.

## Justification
Cost×risk×value: one observed incident to date, cost bounded (~$5-8) and self-correcting (the
session's own end-of-run `git fetch` caught it, and the redundant run paid for itself per
ISSUE-106.md:6). The claim protocol's cost is asymmetric-worse: it needs issue-write permission
"dispatch instructions sometimes withhold" (ISSUE-106.md:15) — mandating it fails exactly where
session isolation is weakest — and a session that dies mid-run leaves a stale claim, blocking
legitimate work with a *worse*, harder-to-notice failure than the rare duplicate spend it
prevents. CLAUDE.md:21 ("don't gold-plate — premature process machinery is itself muda") argues
against building it on a single-incident base rate. Extending the existing precedent
(CLAUDE.md's post-merge fetch-before-ranging rule, now CLAUDE.md:53-57) to a second checkpoint is
near-zero marginal cost and needs no new mechanism, permission, or artifact lifecycle.

## Assumptions
- [verified] single incident to date, bounded/self-correcting cost — ISSUE-106.md:6.
- [verified] no dispatch/backlog skill exists in this repo to host a scripted sync gate — grep of
  `skills/*/SKILL.md` and README.md turned up no dispatch surface; the mechanism has no shipped
  home today (distinct from ADR 0038's shipped-vs-repo-only axis: this rule governs how sessions
  work *this* repo, the same category as the existing PR-shipping bullets already in CLAUDE.md,
  not a portable skill behavior).
- [checkable] CLAUDE.md stays under its 6,000-char budget after the addition — result: verified,
  5,979 chars (`wc -m`).
- [unverifiable] the strengthened two-checkpoint prose rule actually prevents recurrence (depends
  on session compliance, same as every other CLAUDE.md rule) — REOPEN-IF: a second duplicate-spend
  incident occurs despite both checkpoints.

## Rejected alternatives
- **Claim protocol** (issue comment on start/clear on completion) — permission-gated per
  ISSUE-106.md:15, and a crashed session's stale claim is a worse failure mode (false lock on
  legitimate work) than the incident it prevents.
- **Both** (sync + claim) — inherits the claim protocol's risk for no added benefit over the
  strengthened sync rule alone at current (single-incident) frequency.
- **New in-repo lock file/artifact** (not in ISSUE-106.md's list, considered here) — same
  create/clear lifecycle risk as the claim protocol, plus a net-new artifact class this repo
  doesn't otherwise track; work-state belongs in issues, not a repo-local mirror.

## Revisit triggers
- A second duplicate-spend incident occurs despite the two-checkpoint sync rule.
- Multi-session backlog dispatch becomes a standing, frequent practice rather than incidental —
  at that volume the claim protocol's prevention value likely outweighs its permission/staleness
  cost.
- A dispatch/backlog skill is added to this repo, giving the sync check a natural shipped home to
  graduate from prose reminder to an actual scripted gate.
